title: Designing LLM Agent Evaluation Pipelines with Regression Detection
summary: A structured evaluation framework for LLM agent systems: metric selection per agent role, golden eval set construction, baseline snapshot, automated regression detection, and failure triage. Derived from KB-AG-1 and KB-AG-2: evaluation runs against the pipeline_spec and execution_plan to detect regressions when either changes. The trace events from KB-AG-1 instrumentation_spec and the task graph from KB-AG-2 are the primary triage inputs.
preconditions: An agent pipeline exists (KB-AG-1 pipeline_spec) and produces outputs that can be compared against expected results.
An execution plan exists (KB-AG-2 execution_plan) or at least one of: task success criteria, output schema, or human-rated golden examples.

cost_estimate.notes: Golden set construction (step 2) and failure triage (step 6) require human judgment. Metric computation, regression detection, and CI report generation are fully automatable.

steps.action: Select one primary metric per agent role from the pipeline_spec. Map each role to its metric using the role-to-metric guide below.
steps.rationale: Evaluating all possible metrics creates noise and obscures signal. One primary metric per role forces clarity about what each agent is actually optimising for — and makes regressions unambiguous. Secondary metrics are allowed but must not gate CI.

tooling.automated: Role-to-metric mapping: Planner → plan_completeness (fraction of original goal covered by leaf tasks), plan_depth (decomposition levels used). Executor → task_success_rate (atomic tasks completed / total), tool_call_accuracy (correct tool selected / total calls), latency_p95_ms. Critic → precision (true_rejections / total_rejections), recall (caught_failures / total_failures). Router → routing_accuracy (correct_dispatch / total_dispatches). Aggregator → output_coherence_score (0.0-1.0 via LLM judge), hallucination_rate (fabricated_claims / total_claims).
tooling.manual: For each role in pipeline_spec.role_assignment_map: ask 'what is the one number that best measures whether this agent is doing its job?' That is the primary metric. Document the measurement procedure — how is the metric computed from the agent's output?

steps.action: Build the golden eval set: 20-50 representative tasks with human-verified expected outputs, edge cases, and known failure modes. Distribute as 60% typical, 25% edge, 15% adversarial.
steps.rationale: An eval set without edge cases and adversarial inputs passes easily on typical inputs while hiding regressions on boundary conditions — which is exactly where real failures occur. The 60/25/15 distribution ensures the eval set has enough coverage to catch both capability regressions and robustness regressions.

tooling.automated: Eval entry schema: { id: string, input: object, expected_output: object, metric_targets: { [metric_name]: number | boolean }, tags: string[], category: 'typical' | 'edge' | 'adversarial' }. Adversarial examples: prompt injection attempts ('Ignore previous instructions and...'), malformed inputs, out-of-scope requests, inputs at the atomicity boundary from KB-AG-2.
tooling.manual: Seed the eval set from three sources: (1) the execution_plan from KB-AG-2 — use the leaf task descriptions as typical inputs, (2) recent production incidents — use the failing inputs as edge and adversarial cases, (3) the failure_conditions from this KB and its parents — construct inputs that would trigger each failure condition.

steps.action: Establish the baseline snapshot: run the current agent pipeline against the full golden_eval_set and record all metric scores, tagged with a version identifier.
steps.rationale: Regression detection requires a reference point. Without a versioned baseline, every eval run is a fresh measurement with no historical comparison. The baseline must be pinned to a specific agent code version — when the agent changes intentionally, the baseline is re-anchored explicitly, not silently.

tooling.automated: Baseline schema: { version: string, git_sha: string, timestamp: ISO8601, scores: { [metric_name]: { value: number, std_dev: number, sample_size: number } }, eval_set_id: string, pipeline_spec_version: string }. Run the eval set 3 times and record mean ± std_dev to account for LLM non-determinism.
tooling.manual: Baseline anchoring rule: re-anchor only when an agent capability change is intentional and approved. Never re-anchor to suppress a regression — that destroys the integrity of the eval pipeline. Document re-anchoring decisions with: who decided, why, and what capability changed.

steps.action: Set regression thresholds per metric: a regression triggers when a metric drops more than the threshold from the baseline snapshot.
steps.rationale: Thresholds set to zero produce constant false positives from LLM non-determinism. Thresholds set too loose miss genuine regressions. The starting values below are calibrated to be tighter on safety-critical metrics (hallucination, accuracy) and looser on performance metrics (latency) — adjust based on your tolerance.

tooling.automated: Suggested starting thresholds: task_success_rate: -3 percentage points, tool_call_accuracy: -2 percentage points, hallucination_rate: +1 percentage point (absolute), plan_completeness: -5 percentage points, routing_accuracy: -3 percentage points, latency_p95_ms: +20%. Threshold schema: { metric_name, direction: 'increase' | 'decrease', absolute_delta: number, relative_delta: number | null, severity: 'blocking' | 'warning' }.
tooling.manual: Set blocking vs. warning: any metric directly related to correctness or safety (accuracy, hallucination_rate) is blocking — a regression stops the merge. Any metric related to performance or efficiency (latency, cost) is a warning — it surfaces but does not block.

steps.action: Automate the eval run in CI: on every agent code change, run the golden eval set, compute metrics, compare against baseline, and emit a structured report. Gate merge on overall_pass.
steps.rationale: A manual eval process degrades to 'run it before major releases' within weeks. CI integration is what makes regression detection continuous. The structured report — not a human-readable summary — is what downstream triage tooling consumes.

tooling.automated: Eval report schema: { run_id: uuid, timestamp: ISO8601, git_sha: string, pipeline_spec_version: string, metrics: { [name]: { score: number, delta_from_baseline: number, threshold: number, regression: boolean } }, regressions: [{ metric: string, score: number, delta: number, threshold: number, failing_cases: [{ eval_entry_id, input, expected, actual }] }], overall_pass: boolean }. CI gate: fail the pipeline if overall_pass == false AND any regression.severity == 'blocking'.
tooling.manual: Validate the CI gate: introduce a deliberate regression (degrade a mock Executor to fail 10% of tasks). Confirm the CI run reports overall_pass == false and blocks merge. If it passes, the gate is misconfigured.

steps.action: Triage regressions: for each failing case, run the agent in trace mode and use KB-AG-1 instrumentation_spec and KB-AG-2 execution_plan to pinpoint the failing boundary and task node.
steps.rationale: Without structured triage, fixing a regression is guess-and-check. The trace events from KB-AG-1 identify which handoff boundary the failure occurred at. The task graph from KB-AG-2 identifies which decomposition node the failing input maps to. Cross-referencing the two reduces root-cause isolation time significantly versus reading raw logs.

tooling.automated: Triage procedure: (1) for each failing_case in eval_report.regressions: re-run with trace_mode=true, (2) find the trace event where output_hash diverges from the expected value, (3) the boundary_id at that event is the failure location, (4) look up that boundary_id in pipeline_spec.handoff_contracts to find which agent is responsible, (5) look up the input in execution_plan to find the task node — that is the scope of the fix.
tooling.manual: Triage escalation rule: if the same boundary_id appears in > 50% of failing cases, the failure is systemic (agent model change, prompt change, tool schema change). If boundary_ids are scattered, the failure is a data distribution shift in the golden eval set — add more representative examples.

steps.action: Maintain the golden eval set: add failing production cases, remove stale cases, re-baseline only after approved intentional capability changes.
steps.rationale: An eval set that never grows converges to measuring only the patterns present at initial construction. Production incidents are the highest-signal source of new eval cases — they represent real failures the eval set did not anticipate. Re-baselining without approval is the most common way eval integrity is quietly eroded.

tooling.automated: Maintenance schedule: after every production incident, add the incident input to the golden_eval_set as an adversarial case. After 30 days, review cases with eval_entry.category == 'typical' that have never failed — if the task is no longer in scope, remove it. Track eval_set_size over time: a shrinking eval set is a risk signal.
tooling.manual: Monthly review checklist: (1) are all typical cases still representative of current production inputs? (2) has the distribution of production inputs shifted? (3) are the metric targets in expected_output still correct given any capability changes? Update and commit changes with a review justification comment.

successConditions: Every agent role in pipeline_spec.role_assignment_map maps to at least one primary metric in metric_map
golden_eval_set contains >= 20 entries with distribution: >= 50% typical, >= 20% edge, >= 10% adversarial
baseline_snapshot is versioned, pinned to a git_sha, and records mean ± std_dev over >= 3 runs
regression_thresholds are set for every metric in metric_map with blocking vs. warning classification
eval_report is generated automatically on every agent code change
CI gate blocks merge on overall_pass == false with at least one blocking regression
failureConditions: Metric has no measurable definition — delta against baseline cannot be computed
golden_eval_set has < 20 entries or no adversarial cases — insufficient to detect boundary regressions
Baseline re-anchored without documented approval — regressions become undetectable
Regression thresholds set to 0 or disabled for all metrics — pipeline never triggers
CI gate not configured — eval runs but does not block merges

inputs.name: pipeline_spec
inputs.description: Output of KB-AG-1: role_assignment_map, handoff_contracts, instrumentation_spec

inputs.name: execution_plan
inputs.description: Output of KB-AG-2: topologically sorted task graph with role assignments

inputs.name: production_incidents
inputs.description: Optional: recent production failures to seed adversarial eval cases

outputs.name: eval_pipeline_spec
outputs.description: Complete evaluation pipeline: metric_map, golden_eval_set, baseline_snapshot, regression_thresholds, CI gate config

outputs.name: baseline_snapshot
outputs.description: Initial metric scores across all metrics, versioned and pinned to git_sha

outputs.name: eval_report_schema
outputs.description: JSON schema for automated eval reports, ready for CI integration
